Okay, so after you've done the interviewing and you've spoken with the candidates, what you really need to do is take a moment to self-reflect, okay? And so this is something that I actually do every time I interview someone, I go through these questions and I write down my answers and oftentimes it's not me hiring them anymore, so I'm providing the feedback to my team. But either way, it is self-reflective and it will probably bring you to the conclusion that you need to make of if this person is truly the right fit or not, okay? So question one that I ask myself is, is this someone I genuinely enjoy talking to? If I'm going to be spending most of my time with them or someone close to you is going to be spending most of their time with them, do you enjoy being around them? Do you get good vibes, right? Do you feel good talking to them? How did you feel after the conversation was completed, right? Two, does the thought of hiring this person actually excite you? Do you feel excited? Do you feel like they would add value to the team? Are you like at the edge of your seat like, oh my gosh, I really need this person on my team, right? It's not always that much excitement, but genuinely, does it excite you? Do you feel, can you see them doing the job well, right? Question three, what are this person's strengths, okay? So thinking to yourself, what are their strengths? And I would probably be writing them down and then I'm looking at the role and it's like, okay, are there strengths, the strengths needed to do this role, right? Which leads to question four, is this role the best fit for a person given their strengths or is there another role that would be a best fit whether here or elsewhere? Because what you need to remember is that people have to come first. So what that means is that if they're telling you what their strengths are and it doesn't align with an open position you have right now, you can't hire them. Like you need to let them go somewhere else because otherwise it would be a dead end, right? Question five, financially, does the role make sense for both of you? And the candidate and fulfill basically, does it fulfill the business's needs and the prospect's needs, right? So is it going to create financial stability for them and is it going to make sense for you as a business and not take away from what you're taking home, right? And then question six that I asked myself is would I want to work for this person? So especially with a very small business, every person that you bring in is vital, right? And so thinking to yourself, would you want to work for them? This is important whether they are an individual contributor or a leader, but especially if they're a leader. An operation manager or anything, would you want to work for them? And so I write down these questions, I put down my answers to them and I mull it over and then make a decision and you're not going to make it the right decision every time, but this will help you avoid like 70 to 80% of mishirings because a lot of the reason that people hire the wrong people is they go too fast through the process. They don't think about it enough and they don't have enough space and time created into the process to let themselves reflect and really identify red flags, identify things that won't work in the future. So I really suggest you guys do this.
